title: The Config Loader — IDisposable Resource Leak and Null Reference
guidelines: The response MUST identify that `FileStream` and `StreamReader` implement `IDisposable` and are never disposed, causing file handle leaks
Should explain the `using` statement/declaration pattern — `using var` ensures `Dispose()` is called when the variable goes out of scope, even if an exception is thrown
Must identify that `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<T>()` can return null (e.g., for JSON `"null"` or empty content), and the returned value is used without validation
The fix must add `using` to both stream and reader AND add a null check after deserialization
Bonus: mentions that the GC does NOT call `Dispose()` — it may eventually call the finalizer, but relying on that is incorrect and non-deterministic

responses.label: Response A
Response B
Response C

dimensions.name: Root Cause Identification
dimensions.description: Does it identify both bugs: missing IDisposable disposal AND null reference from Deserialize?

dimensions.name: Explanation Depth
dimensions.description: Does it explain the IDisposable pattern, why GC doesn't call Dispose, and when Deserialize returns null?

dimensions.name: Fix Correctness
dimensions.description: Does the fix use `using` statements AND validate the deserialized result?

dimensions.name: Exception Safety
dimensions.description: Does it explain that `using` ensures disposal even when exceptions are thrown (unlike manual Close)?

dimensions.name: Educational Value
dimensions.description: Would this help a developer always use `using` for IDisposable and validate external data?

title: The User Cache — async void and LINQ Deferred Execution
guidelines: The response MUST identify that `async void` prevents callers from catching exceptions — exceptions in async void methods propagate to the SynchronizationContext and crash the application
Should explain the difference between `async void` and `async Task`: Task allows await/catch, void is fire-and-forget with no error handling
Must identify that `users.Where(u => u.IsActive)` stores a deferred query (IEnumerable), not a materialized result — it re-executes every time it's enumerated
The fix must change `async void` to `async Task` AND materialize the LINQ query with `.ToList()`
Bonus: notes that `async void` is only appropriate for event handlers and explains the LINQ deferred execution model

responses.label: Response A
Response B
Response C

dimensions.name: Root Cause Identification
dimensions.description: Does it identify both bugs: async void exception behavior AND LINQ deferred execution?

dimensions.name: Explanation Depth
dimensions.description: Does it explain how async void exceptions propagate to SynchronizationContext and how LINQ defers execution?

dimensions.name: Fix Correctness
dimensions.description: Does the fix change to async Task AND materialize the LINQ query?

dimensions.name: Best Practices
dimensions.description: Does it note that async void is only for event handlers and explain when to materialize LINQ?

dimensions.name: Educational Value
dimensions.description: Would this help a developer avoid async void and understand deferred vs eager evaluation?

title: The Data Processor — C++ Memory Leak and Raw Pointer Ownership
guidelines: The response MUST identify the memory leak: `new int[count]` allocates heap memory that the caller never `delete[]`s, causing unbounded growth
Should explain RAII (Resource Acquisition Is Initialization) — the modern C++ principle that resources should be managed by objects whose destructors automatically clean up
Must identify the null pointer dereference: `*outCount = 0` and `*outCount = count` crash if `outCount` is nullptr (undefined behavior)
The fix should replace raw `new`/`delete` with `std::vector<int>` returned by value, eliminating the ownership ambiguity entirely
Bonus: references the C++ Core Guidelines (R.20–R.24) and explains why returning raw owning pointers is an anti-pattern in modern C++

responses.label: Response A
Response B
Response C

dimensions.name: Root Cause Identification
dimensions.description: Does it identify all bugs: memory leak (missing delete[]), null pointer UB, and unclear ownership?

dimensions.name: Explanation Depth
dimensions.description: Does it explain RAII, why raw new/delete is error-prone, and the C++ Core Guidelines?

dimensions.name: Fix Correctness
dimensions.description: Does the fix use std::vector (RAII) instead of raw pointers, eliminating the leak and ownership ambiguity?

dimensions.name: Modern C++ Practices
dimensions.description: Does it recommend RAII types (vector, unique_ptr) and reference Core Guidelines against raw new/delete?

dimensions.name: Educational Value
dimensions.description: Would this help a developer understand RAII and always prefer value types over raw pointer ownership?
